q: Tell me about a time you had to align multiple senior stakeholders with conflicting priorities on a program. How did you manage it?
star: S: Set context — org size, number of stakeholders, what was at stake
T: Your specific ownership (not just involvement)
A: Concrete steps — 1:1s, data, framework you used, trade-offs surfaced
R: Measurable outcome — decision made, timeline met, relationship preserved
model: Strong answers name the stakeholders, describe the conflicting goals clearly, explain the framework used (e.g. DACI, data-backed trade-off analysis), and quantify the outcome — e.g. "reduced sign-off cycle from 6 weeks to 2 weeks."

q: How are you incorporating AI or automation into your TPM practice? What is one process you have made smarter with technology?
star: S: What the manual/slow process was
T: Your initiative to improve it
A: What tool/AI approach you applied, how you implemented it
R: Time saved, quality improved, scale achieved
model: Strong answers are specific about the before/after — not just "I use AI tools." The best TPMs describe a concrete workflow that changed (e.g. automated status reporting, anomaly detection in program health data) and quantify the impact in hours saved or errors caught.

q: A principal engineer at Google proposes a massive architectural shift for a Trust & Safety system. It supports global scale but comes where the underlying architecture required a total rewrite. How do you, as a TPM, evaluate this?
star: S: The proposed architecture vs the business need
T: The TPM role in evaluating technical trade-offs (build vs buy, latency vs cost)
A: Forcing function discussions, driving design docs (RFC/ADR)
R: A data-backed architectural decision was reached
model: The best TPMs emphasize that they do not make the technical decision alone, but they FORCE the right trade-off conversations regarding the where the underlying architecture required a total rewrite so leadership can decide.
